section.title: Consecration of the Firstborn
section.sectionRationale: Before Israel takes a single step toward the sea, the LORD claims the firstborn as His own and fixes the Feast of Unleavened Bread as a permanent, embodied lesson for the generations to come. Redemption is meant to be taught, worn, and remembered, not merely experienced once.
section.thesis: Because the LORD spared Israel's firstborn at the cost of Egypt's, every firstborn now belongs to Him, redeemed rather than kept, and the whole family is to carry the story of that mighty deliverance on their hands, on their foreheads, and onto their children.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the command to consecrate every firstborn; Moses' instructions for keeping the Feast of Unleavened Bread as a sign and teaching moment; and the specific rule for redeeming the firstborn of donkeys and of sons.

units.label: Consecrate the firstborn
units.note: every firstborn belongs to the LORD

verses.text: Then the LORD said to Moses,
“{{consecrate|Consecrate}} to Me every firstborn male. The firstborn from every womb among the Israelites belongs to Me, both of man and beast.”

units.label: Remember this day
units.note: unleavened bread, and a sign taught to the children

verses.text: So Moses told the people, “Remember this day, the day you came out of Egypt, out of the house of slavery; for the LORD brought you out of it by the strength of His hand. And nothing leavened shall be eaten.
Today, in the month of Abib, you are leaving.

And when the LORD brings you into the land of the Canaanites, Hittites, Amorites, Hivites, and Jebusites, the land He swore to your fathers that He would give you, a land flowing with milk and honey, you shall keep this service in this month.
For seven days you are to eat unleavened bread, and on the seventh day there shall be a feast to the LORD.
Unleavened bread shall be eaten during those seven days. Nothing leavened may be found among you, nor shall leaven be found anywhere within your borders.
And on that day you are to explain to your son, ‘This is because of what the LORD did for me when I came out of Egypt.’
It shall be a {{sign|sign}} for you on your hand and a reminder on your forehead that the Law of the LORD is to be on your lips. For with a mighty hand the LORD brought you out of Egypt.
Therefore you shall keep this statute at the appointed time year after year.

units.label: Redeeming the firstborn
units.note: a lamb for the donkey, a son redeemed

verses.text: And after the LORD brings you into the land of the Canaanites and gives it to you, as He swore to you and your fathers,
you are to present to the LORD the firstborn male of every womb. All the firstborn males of your livestock belong to the LORD.
You must {{redeem|redeem}} every firstborn donkey with a lamb, and if you do not redeem it, you are to break its neck. And every firstborn of your sons you must redeem.
In the future, when your son asks you, ‘What does this mean?’ you are to tell him, ‘With a mighty hand the LORD brought us out of Egypt, out of the house of slavery.

And when Pharaoh stubbornly refused to let us go, the LORD killed every firstborn in the land of Egypt, both of man and beast. This is why I sacrifice to the LORD the firstborn male of every womb, but I redeem all the firstborn of my sons.’
So it shall serve as a sign on your hand and a symbol on your forehead, for with a mighty hand the LORD brought us out of Egypt.”

consecrate.gloss: To set apart as holy, to dedicate to God's exclusive use. The firstborn already belonged to the LORD in principle since He spared them in Egypt; now Israel is told to formally set them apart, acknowledging in ritual what is already true.

sign.gloss: A sign, a visible marker of a deeper reality. Later Jewish tradition took the command to bind this as a sign on the hand and forehead quite literally, in tefillin; whether literal or figurative, the point is that redemption must be kept visibly present before the worshiper.

redeem.gloss: To buy back, to ransom, to release by paying a price or substitute. A firstborn donkey is redeemed with a lamb or its neck is broken; a firstborn son is always redeemed, never sacrificed, because in Israel human life is never the payment, only pictured by a substitute.

basic.context: Right after Israel leaves Egypt, before they even reach the sea, God tells Moses that every firstborn now belongs to Him, because He spared Israel's firstborn at the cost of Egypt's. Along with that comes the Feast of Unleavened Bread, a yearly reminder they are to explain to their children.
basic.hermeneutics: This describes actual instructions given to Israel for their ongoing worship and family teaching. Christians are not bound to keep the Feast of Unleavened Bread or redeem literal firstborn sons, but the underlying principle endures: God's mighty acts of redemption are to be remembered, taught to children, and kept visibly present in daily life.
basic.originalLanguages: To qadash, consecrate, the firstborn is to set them apart as belonging to God alone, acknowledging what His deliverance already made true. The instruction to wear this as an ot, a sign, on hand and forehead insists that redemption not stay a private memory but shape daily, visible life. And to padah, redeem, a firstborn is to substitute something else, a lamb or a price, in its place.
basic.typology: Every firstborn son in Israel is redeemed, never sacrificed, because a substitute always stands in his place, just as the Passover lamb once stood in the place of Israel's firstborn in Egypt. This repeated pattern of substitution prepares the way for the ultimate substitute, Christ, who redeems His people at the cost of His own life.
basic.christ: Luke's Gospel deliberately echoes this chapter when Mary and Joseph present the infant Jesus in the temple to fulfill the law of the firstborn, Luke 2:22-23. Jesus, the true firstborn, is not redeemed by a substitute but becomes the substitute Himself, the price paid so that many sons and daughters are set free.
basic.god: God claims what He has already saved; He does not merely rescue and move on but insists that the rescued live as people who belong to Him. He is also endlessly patient with the ordinary work of teaching, expecting parents to explain and children to ask, generation after generation.
basic.crossReferences: Luke 2:22-23 shows Jesus presented according to this very law of the firstborn. Numbers 3:11-13 later assigns the Levites to stand in the place of Israel's firstborn. Deuteronomy 6:6-9 echoes the same command to bind God's words on hand and forehead and teach them to children.
basic.oneStory: God claims a redeemed people as His own and commands that their story be worn, spoken, and passed on, not simply remembered in private. This becomes the shape of the whole Bible's call to the redeemed: live visibly as those who belong to God, and teach the next generation the mighty hand that set you free.

context.literary: The chapter pairs a short command, consecrate the firstborn, with an extended explanation of how the Feast of Unleavened Bread functions as a teaching tool, then closes by circling back to the specific mechanics of redeeming firstborn animals and sons.
context.historical: This law shapes Israel's ongoing worship long after the exodus itself, later assigning the tribe of Levi to serve in place of the firstborn, Numbers 3, and it stands behind the practice, whether literal or symbolic, of binding God's words on hand and forehead that later Judaism developed as tefillin.
context.canonical: This is one of several places in the Torah, alongside Deuteronomy 6, that ties God's redemptive acts to visible, embodied signs and to deliberate teaching of children, establishing a pattern of remembered grace that runs through the rest of Scripture.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To establish that the firstborn belong to the LORD because He spared them, and to build permanent, repeated occasions, a feast and a visible sign, for teaching each new generation the meaning of their redemption from Egypt.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This passage prescribes specific ceremonial law for Israel under the old covenant, the Feast of Unleavened Bread and the redemption of firstborn animals and sons; Christians are not bound to these ceremonial forms, but the underlying principle, that redemption must be remembered, taught, and visibly lived out, carries forward as an abiding truth.

debates.question: Should the sign on the hand and frontlets on the forehead be taken literally or figuratively?

views.label: A literal, physical practice
views.case: Later Jewish tradition developed tefillin, small boxes containing Scripture bound to the hand and forehead, reading the command with concrete literalism.

views.label: A vivid figure of speech
views.case: Many interpreters, noting the parallel phrase in Deuteronomy 6:6-9 about writing the law on doorposts, read this as figurative language urging that God's redemption govern one's actions, thoughts, and speech, rather than a command for a physical object.

debates.bearing: Either way, the text insists that redemption is not meant to remain an abstract memory but to visibly shape a life; the debate is only over the literal mechanism, not the underlying demand.

study.originalLanguages: The command to qadash, consecrate, every firstborn recognizes formally what God's deliverance already accomplished, that the firstborn belongs to Him. The ot, sign, on hand and forehead insists that this redemption stay visibly before the worshiper's eyes and hands, shaping both thought and action. And every firstborn is padah, redeemed, substituted for, never simply kept or sacrificed as personal property.

typology.type: The redeemed firstborn son, never sacrificed but substituted for
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the true firstborn, who is Himself the substitute rather than one who needs redeeming, Colossians 1:15, 18.

typology.type: The Levites standing in the place of Israel's firstborn, Numbers 3:11-13
typology.fulfillment: A picture of a whole people set apart to serve, fulfilled in the church as a kingdom of priests belonging to Christ, 1 Peter 2:9.

study.christ: Luke 2:22-23 shows the infant Jesus brought to the temple precisely to fulfill this law of the firstborn. Yet the deeper pattern is greater than the ceremony: every other firstborn in Israel needed a substitute to be redeemed, but Jesus, the true and greater firstborn, needed no redemption Himself and instead becomes the redemption price for everyone else, Mark 10:45.
study.god: God is a God who claims what He has saved; having spared Israel's firstborn, He does not leave the matter there but calls them formally His own. He is also a patient teacher, building into Israel's calendar and family life repeated, unavoidable occasions for children to ask and parents to explain the story of their redemption.

crossReferences.note: Jesus is presented in the temple in direct fulfillment of this law of the firstborn.
The Levites are later set apart to serve in the place of Israel's firstborn.
The same language of binding God's words on hand and forehead and teaching children reappears here.
Christ is called the firstborn over all creation and firstborn from the dead, the true and ultimate firstborn.

study.oneStory: God claims the firstborn He has spared and builds permanent, embodied ways for Israel to remember and teach their redemption. This anticipates the whole Bible's pattern: God saves a people for Himself and calls them to carry that story visibly, on their lives and to their children, until the true and greater firstborn, Christ, redeems a people forever.

quiz.q: Why does the LORD claim every firstborn as belonging to Him, according to this chapter?

options.text: Because He spared Israel's firstborn in Egypt when He struck down Egypt's firstborn.
options.note: Verses 14 and 15 give exactly this reason, tying the claim directly to the Passover deliverance.

options.text: Because firstborn children were considered unlucky in the ancient world.
options.note: The text gives a clear redemptive reason, not a superstition about luck.

options.text: Because Israel had more children than they could support.
options.note: The reason given is theological, tied to the exodus, not a matter of family size.

options.text: No reason is given in the text.
options.note: Verses 14 to 15 explicitly explain the reason, rooted in the Passover.

quiz.q: What must be done with a firstborn donkey, according to verse 13?

options.text: It must be redeemed with a lamb, or else its neck must be broken.
options.note: The text gives this precise alternative, redemption or a broken neck.

options.text: It must be sacrificed on the altar like any other firstborn animal.
options.note: The donkey specifically cannot simply be sacrificed; it must be redeemed or killed by breaking its neck, unlike a clean sacrificial animal.

options.text: It must be sold to a foreigner.
options.note: Selling is not the instruction given; redemption with a lamb is the stated path.

options.text: Nothing needs to be done with it.
options.note: The text is explicit that it must be redeemed or its neck broken.

quiz.q: What was to happen with every firstborn son, according to verses 13 to 15?

options.text: He must always be redeemed, never sacrificed.
options.note: Unlike animals which could be substituted with a lamb or killed, human firstborn sons were always to be redeemed.

options.text: He was to be sacrificed as an offering to the LORD.
options.note: The text explicitly rules this out; the son is redeemed, not sacrificed, distinguishing Israel from surrounding child-sacrifice practices.

options.text: He was to be given away to a Levite family.
options.note: That is not what this text instructs; redemption, likely through payment or substitution, is in view here.

options.text: Nothing was required for firstborn sons.
options.note: The text explicitly requires redemption of every firstborn son.

quiz.q: What purpose does the sign on the hand and the reminder on the forehead serve?

options.text: To keep the Law of the LORD on the people's lips and the memory of the exodus ever before them.
options.note: Verse 9 states this purpose explicitly.

options.text: To identify Israelites to Egyptian border guards.
options.note: The text gives a devotional and teaching purpose, not a practical identification function.

options.text: To ward off future plagues.
options.note: No such protective function is described; the purpose is remembrance and teaching.

options.text: The text gives no explanation for the sign.
options.note: Verse 9 gives a clear explanation.

quiz.q: How does the New Testament connect to this chapter's law of the firstborn?

options.text: Jesus is presented in the temple as an infant to fulfill this very law.
options.note: Luke 2:22-23 draws a direct line to this passage.

options.text: The New Testament never mentions the firstborn at all.
options.note: Luke's Gospel explicitly cites this law in the presentation of Jesus.

options.text: Paul rejects this law as having no ongoing significance.
options.note: Paul does not reject its significance; he draws on firstborn language to describe Christ, Colossians 1:15, 18.

options.text: It is connected only to Old Testament priestly practice with no bearing on Christ.
options.note: Luke and Colossians both draw meaningful theological lines from this law to Christ.

questions: God claimed Israel's firstborn because He had already spared them. What has God already saved in your life that you have not yet formally set apart for Him?

Israel was told to explain their redemption to their children, again and again, year after year. How intentional are you about telling the story of what God has done for you to the people who come after you?

Every firstborn son was redeemed, never sacrificed, because a substitute stood in his place. How does it change your sense of security to know Christ, the true firstborn, became the substitute so you never have to be one?
The sign was meant to be on the hand and before the eyes, impossible to forget in the course of an ordinary day. What daily habit or reminder keeps God's redemption visibly before you?

This whole chapter turns a past rescue into a present and future obligation of worship. Where is God calling you to move from simply remembering what He did to actively living differently because of it?

translationNotes.note: A sign on your hand and a reminder on your forehead is figurative language in its immediate context, closely paralleled in Deuteronomy 6:8, though later Jewish practice developed it into the literal wearing of tefillin.

Break its neck describes the alternative to redemption for an unclean animal like a donkey, which could not be offered as a sacrifice; this preserved the owner's obligation to acknowledge the LORD's claim on the firstborn even without a formal offering.
Symbol on your forehead uses different Hebrew wording than verse 9's reminder, though both describe the same enduring, visible memorial of the exodus.
